one_line_takeaway: A compact (0.8B-2B) parallel guard model, trained on a large web-injection dataset plus guard-targeted and adaptive-adversarial fine-tuning, achieves near-perfect prompt-injection detection for web agents with ~0.25% false-positive rate and strong robustness to guard-targeted and adaptive attacks.
key_finding: WARD dominates prior guard models across all four axes. Detection (Table 1): WARD-0.8B reaches 99.33% accuracy / 99.34% F1 on WARD-Test (WARD-2B 99.20% on both), and both models achieve 100% recall on the four OOD benchmarks (Popup, EIA, VPI, WASP), versus the best baseline WebAgentGuard-8b at 85.20% recall on WARD-Test. PIG robustness (Table 2): WARD-0.8B and WARD-2B hold 100% recall across all benchmarks and modalities (HTML/screenshot/both), while WebAgentGuard-4b/8b collapse to as low as 2.50% and their own ablated WARD-w/o-PIG variants drop substantially (e.g. 17.32%). Cross-domain adaptive (PIArena, Table 3): WARD-0.8B/2B average per-sample ASR of only 3.23%/3.08%, versus PromptArmor 92.21%, PromptGuard 92.14%, DataSentinel 45.71%. Utility (Table 4): average FPR 0.26% (0.8B) / 0.25% (2B) with performance degradation 0.37%/0.25%, versus WebAgentGuard-4b/8b at 7.17%/13.12% FPR. Deployed with agents on VPI, WARD reduces attack success rate to 0%. In-domain adaptive (A3T, Figure 4): in the final training cycle SSR stays near zero at trial budget 5 and rises only to 3.12 (WARD-2B) / 5.62 (WARD-0.8B) at budget 10, with attempt-success-rate maxing at 2.65% (2B) / 3.69% (0.8B), while WebAgentGuard reaches ~74-79% SSR. Efficiency: WARD uses ~128-152 output tokens/sample versus 400-469 for baselines and runs faster than the agent itself.
core_contribution: WARD (Web Agent Robust Defense against Prompt Injection) is a guard-model framework that runs in parallel with a web agent to detect prompt-injection attacks embedded in webpage HTML and/or screenshots before the agent acts. The paper's own contributions are three: (1) WARD-Base, a ~177K-sample training dataset collected from 719 high-traffic URLs/platforms via a two-branch pipeline (an "overlay" branch that injects attacks into real crawled HTML/screenshots, and a "native" branch that embeds attacks into user-generated content on high-risk platforms like social media/messaging); (2) WARD-PIG, a dataset that adds guard-targeted "prompt injection on guard" (PIG) prompts so the model learns to treat guard-facing instructions as adversarial rather than obey them; and (3) A3T (Adaptive Adversarial Attack Training), an iterative attacker-guard co-evolution scheme where a memory-based attacker plus a validator generate attacks that bypass the current guard, and successful attacks update the guard via GRPO. The released guards are compact Qwen-3.5-0.8B and Qwen-3.5-2B models. Note: the threat categories AAA (adaptive adversarial attacks) and PIG are cited prior concepts the paper defends against, not its inventions; A3T and the two datasets are the paper's methods.
threat_model: Web agents interact with open web content and can be hijacked by prompt-injection instructions embedded in a page's HTML or in its visual/screenshot interface, causing unsafe actions such as data leakage or unauthorized operations. The paper additionally considers two adversary escalations against the defense itself: Prompt Injection on Guard (PIG), where the attacker embeds instructions (e.g. fake benign ground-truth labels in the guard's JSON output format) to manipulate the guard's decision process; and adaptive adversarial attacks (AAA), where the attacker iteratively observes and rewrites prompts to bypass the guard over multiple trials. The defender is a separate guard model inspecting webpage state (HTML + screenshot) before the agent executes each step. Both HTML-only, screenshot-only, and dual-modality injection channels are in scope.
limitations: Evaluation is confined to the paper's own training/eval ecosystem and closely related benchmarks from largely overlapping author groups (WARD-Base, VPI, WebAgentGuard, WASP, EIA, Popup, PIArena), which may inflate apparent generalization; several near-100% results (100% OOD recall, 100% PIG recall, 0% deployment ASR) risk being close to a ceiling and hard to distinguish from benchmark saturation. The A3T stress test itself uses the same attack framework the model was trained against, and the authors acknowledge (Appendix E) qualitative failure cases where WARD can still be bypassed at higher trial budgets (SSR rises to 3-5% at budget 10). The guard adds a second model that must inspect every step; while shown efficient on a single H200, real deployment cost/latency at scale is not deeply analyzed. Full training configs and ablations are relegated to appendices (Appendix B), beyond the 22-page extraction reviewed here.

datasets_used: WARD-Base (~177K samples, 719 URLs/platforms; the paper's own training set)
WARD-PIG (guard-targeted prompt-injection dataset; ~10,500 malicious + 10,500 benign samples; the paper's own)
PIArena (cross-domain adaptive attack, 1,700 samples across 13 datasets incl. SQuAD, Dolly, NQ, MSMARCO, HotpotQA, Qasper, GovReport, MultiNews, LCC)
quantitative_results: WARD-0.8B: 99.33% accuracy, 99.34% F1 on WARD-Test; WARD-2B: 99.20% accuracy and F1
Both WARD models: 100% recall on OOD benchmarks Popup, EIA, VPI, WASP
PIG robustness: WARD-0.8B and WARD-2B maintain 100% recall across all benchmarks/modalities; WebAgentGuard-4b drops as low as 2.50%
Cross-domain adaptive (PIArena) average per-sample ASR: WARD-0.8B 3.23%, WARD-2B 3.08%, vs PromptArmor 92.21%, PromptGuard 92.14%, DataSentinel 45.71%
Utility: average FPR 0.26% (WARD-0.8B) / 0.25% (WARD-2B) and performance degradation 0.37% / 0.25%, vs WebAgentGuard-4b 7.17% FPR and WebAgentGuard-8b 13.12% FPR
Agent deployment on VPI: WARD reduces attack success rate to 0%
In-domain adaptive (A3T, final cycle, trial budget 10): SSR 3.12% (WARD-2B) / 5.62% (WARD-0.8B); ASR 2.65% (WARD-2B) / 3.69% (WARD-0.8B), vs WebAgentGuard ~74-79% SSR
Efficiency: WARD ~128-152 output tokens/sample vs 400-469 for baselines; runs faster than the agent model
baselines_compared: Closed-source APIs: GPT-5.4, GPT-5.4-Mini, Gemini-3.1-pro, Gemini-3-Flash, Claude-Sonnet-4.6, Claude-Haiku-4.5
Open instructed models: Qwen-3.5-0.8B/2B/4B, Qwen3.5-35B-A3B, Gemma-3-4B, Gemma-4-31B
General safety guards: Llama-Guard-4-12B, Llama-Guard-3-Vision-11B, GPT-Oss-Safeguard-20b, Prompt-Guard-1/2-86M, GuardReasoner-VL-7B
Prompt-injection guards: BrowseSafe, PromptArmor, DataSentinel, SuperAgent-Guard-1.7b/4b, WebAgentGuard-4b/8b
